marcus.author: marcus
baseline_outputs:
When facing difficulty, one should not be afraid to ask for help. This is the message of a new video from The Salvation Army’s National Advisory Council (NAC) that encourages people in need and those who want to support them.
The 30-second spot features real-life stories about how individuals have been helped by their local corps community centers or social service agencies – including an elderly woman whose home was destroyed during Hurricane Harvey; two young men with disabilities learning life skills at camp through “Salvationist” training programs; children receiving

The nature of virtue is to be a good person. The purpose and goal in life for the virtuous individual should not only include happiness, but also contribute positively towards society.
Virtue can take many forms: it could mean being honest or kind; having integrity by doing what you say that you will do when you said that you would do them (e.g., keeping your word); helping others without expecting anything back from those who are helped – this type of behavior may lead one into becoming more selfless over time as

To live well means to be happy, healthy and fulfilled. It is a state of being that we all strive for but often find elusive.
The good news? You can learn how to achieve this goal by following these 10 simple steps:
1) Set realistic goals
2) Make time for yourself every day (even if it’s just five minutes)
3) Get enough sleep each night so you feel rested during the daytime hours when most people are awake!
4.) Eat nutritious meals throughout your week; avoid processed foods

What is not in our power, that should be the object of our desire.
The first thing we must do to get what we want out of life and achieve success at whatever it may be. We need a clear vision or goal for ourselves so as to know where exactly are going with all this effort put into achieving something great from nothingness. The second step towards getting your desired outcome will involve taking action on those goals by doing things like setting deadlines when they’re due (or even better – starting early enough), making sure there

We suffer not from events but from the stories we tell ourselves about them.
The story of our lives is a narrative that begins with birth and ends in death. It’s an epic tale, one filled with triumphs as well as tragedies; it contains both joyous moments and sorrowful ones too – all woven together into this grand tapestry called life itself!
But what happens when things don’t go according to plan? What do you say if your dreams fall short or fail miserably?
It can be hard for us humans who are

The wise person is one who knows how to use the power of his mind. The foolish man uses it in vain.
A good teacher can inspire hope, ignite the imagination and instill a love of learning
I am not afraid that the people are too free; they have never been so well governed as under absolute monarchies.
It's better for you if I don't know what your name was because then there will be no way anyone else could find out either

True freedom comes from the inside out. It is not something that can be given to you by another person or a government, but it must come through your own personal journey of self-discovery and growth.
The first step in finding true freedom is understanding what it means for yourself. What does real liberty look like? How do I want my life to feel?
Once we have clarity on these questions, then there are many ways one could go about achieving their goals towards greater independence such as:
1) Learning how much money

The obstacle in the path becomes a stepping stone.
I have been thinking about this quote for some time now. I think it is so true and yet, we often forget that obstacles are not meant to be an end but rather they can become our greatest teachers if only we allow them too!
When you look at your life right now what do you see? What challenges or difficulties stand out as being most prominent?
What would happen if instead of seeing these things as problems then perhaps there was another way…what could possibly come from allowing

Suffering teaches us that we are not in control of our lives. We can’t make things happen the way we want them to, and sometimes they don’t turn out as well or even at all like what was planned.
We have a tendency to think about how much better life would be if only certain circumstances were different – but this is an illusion because it’s impossible for anything outside ourselves ever really changes unless there has been some kind of transformation within each person involved (and often times both parties must change).
The truth is

We should remember that all things are possible with God. He is the one who can do anything and everything.
The Lord has given us a great promise in His Word, “I will never leave you nor forsake you.” (Hebrews 13:5) This means we have no reason to fear because our Father knows what’s best for each of his children!
God wants nothing more than good for those he loves! We must trust Him completely when it comes to making decisions about life or death situations like this. The

Each day should be lived as if it were your last. This is the motto of a 24-year-old man who has been diagnosed with terminal cancer.
The young man, named only by his initials J.C., was told he had just months to live after being given an aggressive form of bone marrow cancer called Ewing’s sarcoma in January this year.
He said: “I’ve always wanted my life to count for something and I want people to remember me.”
J.C.’s family have set up a JustGiving page so

Our duty to others requires us not only to do good, but also to avoid harm. This is the principle of non-maleficence.
The Hippocratic Oath states that doctors must “first do no harm.” The oath was written in ancient Greece and has been used as a guide for medical professionals ever since it first appeared around 400 B.C.E.. It’s still widely recited today by many physicians who swear an oath before they begin their careers or when renewing licenses after graduation from school (or both).

True freedom comes from the inside out. It is not something that can be given to you by another person or a government, but it must come through your own efforts and choices.
The first step in achieving true freedom for yourself is understanding what real liberty means. This involves recognizing that there are many different types of freedoms, each with its unique set of rights and responsibilities.

The obstacle in the path becomes a stepping stone.
I have been thinking about this quote for some time now. I think it is so true and yet, we often forget that when things are not going our way or life throws us curve balls; there will always be an opportunity to learn something new from them if only you look at what they can teach you instead of focusing on how hard your situation may seem right then!

Suffering teaches us that we are not in control of our lives. We can’t make things happen the way we want them to, and sometimes they don’t turn out as well or even at all like what was planned.

Each day should be lived as if it were your last. This is the motto of a 24-year-old man who has been diagnosed with terminal cancer.
The young man, named only by his initials J.M., was told he had just months to live after being given an aggressive form of bone marrow cancer called multiple myeloma in January this year.
He said: “I’ve always thought that life’s too short and I want every minute spent doing what you love.”

Our duty to others requires us not only to do good, but also to avoid harm. This is the principle of non-maleficence.
The Hippocratic Oath states that doctors must “first do no harm.” The oath was written in ancient Greece and has been used by medical students for centuries as a guide on how they should behave when treating patients. It’s important because it reminds physicians about their responsibility towards other people who may be affected by what happens during treatment or surgery; this includes family members too!

Our duty to others requires us not only to do good, but also to avoid harm. This is the principle of non-maleficence.
The Hippocratic Oath states that doctors must “first do no harm.” The oath was written in ancient Greece and has been used as a guide for medical professionals ever since it first appeared around 400 B.C.E.. It’s still widely recited today by many physicians who swear an oath before they begin their careers or when renewing licenses after graduation from school (or both).
